questions.stem: A 72-year-old man presents with fatigue of 6 months. He drinks 14 units/week. Examination: pale, no lymphadenopathy. FBC: Hb 98 g/L, MCV 106 fL, WCC 5.2×10^9/L, platelets 220×10^9/L. B12 250 ng/L (normal), folate 6.5 µg/L (normal), ferritin 56 µg/L. Blood film shows macrocytes, occasional nucleated RBC. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Macrocytic anaemia with normal B12/folate and no significant alcohol excess points to MDS. Alcohol macrocytosis is usually mild with normal Hb. Aplastic anaemia typically causes pancytopenia. B12/folate deficiencies are excluded by normal levels.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman is found to have Hb 110 g/L, MCV 98 fL, platelets 85×10^9/L, WCC 3.0×10^9/L on routine bloods. She has no symptoms. Examination: no bruises, no organomegaly. B12/folate normal, blood film shows bilobed neutrophils (Pelger-Huet) and occasional blast cells <1%. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Bilobed neutrophils (pseudo-Pelger-Huet) and mild pancytopenia with normal B12/folate are classic for MDS. ITP has isolated thrombocytopenia. Aplastic anaemia is more severe and lacks dysplastic neutrophils. CML has leucocytosis. Myelofibrosis often has splenomegaly and teardrop cells.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man presents with bruising and fever. FBC: Hb 85 g/L, WCC 2.1×10^9/L (neutrophils 0.5), platelets 25×10^9/L. Blood film shows blast cells 12%. Bone marrow: hypercellular, dysplastic changes, blast cells 14%. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Blast count 14% in marrow falls within MDS-EB (5-19% blasts) per WHO. AML requires ≥20% blasts. Aplastic anaemia is hypocellular with no blasts. Myelofibrosis shows fibrosis. Hypoplastic MDS has <5% blasts.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man has Hb 120 g/L, MCV 88 fL, platelets 600×10^9/L, WCC 9.0×10^9/L. Examination: no splenomegaly. Blood film shows large platelets, occasional ring sideroblasts. JAK2 V617F negative, CALR negative, BCR-ABL negative. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Ring sideroblasts with thrombocytosis, negative JAK2/CALR/BCR-ABL, strongly suggest MDS-RS (often SF3B1 mutation). ET requires a positive driver mutation. CML is BCR-ABL positive. PV has raised Hb/Hct. Reactive causes lack dysplastic features.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with fatigue and bruising. She had chemotherapy for breast cancer 3 years ago. FBC: Hb 90 g/L, WCC 2.5, platelets 55. Blood film shows dysplastic neutrophils and blast cells 5%. Bone marrow: hypocellular with dysplasia, blast 6%, no fibrosis. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: History of cytotoxic therapy, pancytopenia, dysplasia and <20% blasts indicate t-MDS. Relapsed cancer would show metastases. Aplastic anaemia lacks dysplasia and blasts. Myelofibrosis has fibrosis. AML requires >20% blasts.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man presents with fatigue. Full blood count shows Hb 95 g/L, MCV 105 fL, reticulocyte count 0.5%. B12 and folate are normal. Peripheral blood film shows macrocytes and occasional nucleated red cells. What is the most appropriate next test to establish the diagnosis?
options: Bone marrow aspirate and trephine biopsy
Repeat full blood count in 3 months
questions.explanation: Bone marrow aspirate with trephine biopsy is the gold standard for diagnosing MDS, demonstrating dysplasia in at least one cell line, ring sideroblasts, or increased blasts (<20%). Peripheral blood film may suggest dysplasia but cannot confirm the diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with no significant past medical history has an incidental finding of isolated thrombocytopenia (platelets 95 x 10^9/L) over 6 months. Peripheral blood film is normal. Bone marrow aspirate shows mild dysplastic changes in megakaryocytes and 2% blasts. Cytogenetics of the marrow is normal. Which investigation is most useful to support a diagnosis of MDS in this case?
options: Repeat bone marrow aspirate in 3 months
Flow cytometry of bone marrow for aberrant markers
Fluorescence in situ hybridisation (FISH) for common MDS abnormalities
questions.explanation: MDS can be confirmed by the presence of a clonal mutation (e.g., SF3B1, TET2, ASXL1) in ~90% of cases with normal cytogenetics. Next-generation sequencing identifies these mutations, providing clonal evidence. FISH may miss small clones.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man presents with fever, pallor, and bruising. Full blood count shows pancytopenia: Hb 80 g/L, WCC 1.2 x 10^9/L, platelets 25 x 10^9/L. Peripheral blood film shows blast cells (5%). A bone marrow biopsy is performed. The aspirate contains 15% blasts. What is the single best interpretation of this result?
questions.explanation: MDS-EB1 is defined by 5–9% blasts in bone marrow. Blast count <20% distinguishes MDS from AML. The peripheral blast count is not the diagnostic criterion; marrow blast percentage is definitive.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with known MDS (isolated del5q on cytogenetics) has been transfusion-dependent for 2 years. Her most recent full blood count shows Hb 82 g/L, WCC 2.0 x 10^9/L, platelets 45 x 10^9/L, and a manual differential shows 6% blasts. A repeat bone marrow biopsy is performed. The aspirate shows 12% blasts, still with del5q, and no new cytogenetic abnormalities. What is the most appropriate diagnostic conclusion?
options: Progression to MDS with excess blasts-2 (MDS-EB2)
questions.explanation: An increase in blasts to 10–19% in bone marrow qualifies as MDS-EB2 (now classified as MDS-IB2 in WHO 2022). The blast count is <20%, so not AML. This indicates progression. The new diagnosis classification reflects the higher risk category.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with a history of Hodgkin lymphoma treated 8 years ago with ABVD chemotherapy now presents with fatigue and easy bruising. Full blood count shows Hb 90 g/L, MCV 100 fL, WCC 2.5 x 10^9/L, platelets 60 x 10^9/L. Peripheral blood film shows macrocytes, occasional dysplastic neutrophils, and 2% blasts. What is the most appropriate initial investigation to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Bone marrow aspirate and trephine biopsy with cytogenetics
questions.explanation: The patient has pancytopenia with dysplasia and a history of chemotherapy, raising suspicion of therapy-related MDS (t-MDS). Bone marrow biopsy with cytogenetics is essential to confirm the diagnosis, assess blast count, and detect characteristic clonal abnormalities (e.g., -7, del5q).

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with lower-risk MDS (IPSS-R low) has symptomatic anaemia (Hb 85 g/L). He has a serum erythropoietin level of 40 U/L and is transfusion-independent. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: For lower-risk MDS with symptomatic anaemia and low serum EPO (<500 U/L), ESAs (epoetin alfa or darbepoetin) are first-line. Transfusion is reserved for severe anaemia or ESA failure; lenalidomide is for del5q; azacitidine for higher-risk; iron chelation for iron overload.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with higher-risk MDS (IPSS-R intermediate-2) has pancytopenia and 12% bone marrow blasts. She is not fit for intensive chemotherapy or allogeneic stem cell transplantation. What is the most appropriate first-line disease-modifying therapy?
questions.explanation: For higher-risk MDS not fit for transplant, azacitidine is the hypomethylating agent of choice (NICE approved). Decitabine is an alternative but less established; lenalidomide is for del5q only; best supportive care is inferior; transplant is not first-line in unfit patients.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with MDS and isolated del(5q) has transfusion-dependent anaemia (Hb 70 g/L). His bone marrow blasts are <5%. What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
questions.explanation: Lenalidomide is the first-line treatment for transfusion-dependent anaemia due to del5q MDS (NICE guidance). ESAs are ineffective in del5q; azacitidine is for higher-risk; transplant is later-line; hydroxycarbamide is not indicated.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old woman with MDS has received over 20 units of red cells and now has serum ferritin >2000 μg/L with cardiac iron overload on MRI. Which therapy is most appropriate to manage transfusion-related iron overload?
questions.explanation: Deferasirox is first-line oral iron chelation for transfusion iron overload in MDS (NICE). Phlebotomy is not feasible in anaemic patients; ascorbic acid can increase iron toxicity; deferiprone is second-line; folic acid does not remove iron.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with MDS (IPSS-R intermediate-1) has severe neutropenia (neutrophils 0.3×10^9/L) and recurrent bacterial infections despite prophylactic antibiotics. What is the most appropriate management?
questions.explanation: G-CSF (filgrastim) is indicated for severe neutropenia with recurrent infections to reduce infection risk. Granulocyte transfusions are rarely used; antibiotics only when febrile is reactive; azacitidine is for higher-risk disease; transplant is too aggressive for this risk group.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old woman presents with 3 months of fatigue and dyspnoea on exertion. Blood results: Hb 85 g/L, MCV 102 fL, WCC 3.2 ×10⁹/L, platelets 95×10⁹/L. Peripheral blood film shows macrocytes and occasional blast cells. What is the next most appropriate step?
options: Refer for urgent red cell transfusion
Start vitamin B12 and folate supplements
Arrange bone marrow aspiration and trephine biopsy
Request serum B12, folate and ferritin levels
questions.explanation: The combination of macrocytic anaemia with peripheral blasts and bicytopenia is highly suspicious for MDS. The definitive diagnostic test is bone marrow examination. Vitamin B12/folate supplementation (options a and e) is a reasonable consideration for macrocytic anaemia but the presence of blasts makes MDS more likely. Transfusion (option c) is symptomatic but not diagnostic. Lenalidomide (option d) is a specific treatment for del(5q) MDS, not first-line without diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with known low-risk MDS (IPSS-R low) and haemoglobin stable at 100 g/L presents with worsening fatigue over 2 weeks. Repeat FBC shows Hb 82 g/L, WCC and platelets unchanged. He has no B symptoms or bleeding. What is the next most appropriate step?
options: Transfuse 2 units of packed red cells
questions.explanation: In low-risk MDS with symptomatic anaemia, ESAs are indicated if serum EPO <500 U/L and no del(5q). Checking EPO level (option d) directs appropriate use of ESA and avoids unnecessary treatment. Starting ESA (option a) without EPO level is premature. Transfusion (option c) is reserved for severe symptoms or ESA failure. Bone marrow examination (option b) is not urgently needed unless progression is suspected; here prior stable disease.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with known MDS with excess blasts (MDS-EB2) is admitted with fever 38.9°C, productive cough, and confusion. HR 110, BP 95/60. Neutrophils 0.4×10⁹/L. Chest X-ray shows right lower lobe consolidation. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Take blood cultures then start antibiotics
questions.explanation: Febrile neutropenia is an emergency requiring broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour. Guidelines mandate blood cultures before antibiotics (option b) to maximise yield. Starting antibiotics without cultures (option a) is inappropriate when cultures can be obtained quickly. Fluids (option c) may be needed but are secondary to antibiotics. CT thorax (option d) delays treatment. G-CSF (option e) is not given in the acute setting.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with MDS with isolated del(5q) on lenalidomide for 6 months presents with Hb 70 g/L (previously 100 g/L), platelets 50×10⁹/L (previously 150×10⁹/L). He feels increasingly breathless. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Transfuse 2 units of packed red cells
questions.explanation: New or worsening cytopenias on lenalidomide suggest drug toxicity or disease progression. The first step is to stop lenalidomide (option a) to assess for recovery. Transfusion (option b) is supportive but does not address the cause. Increasing dose (option c) would worsen toxicity. Bone marrow biopsy (option d) is indicated but stopping the drug takes priority. Erythropoietin (option e) is not indicated and may be harmful.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old woman with MDS and multiple comorbidities presents with symptomatic anaemia (Hb 72 g/L). She is not fit for intensive chemotherapy or stem cell transplant. Bone marrow shows blasts 12% (MDS-EB2). She declines transfusion. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Refer for allogeneic stem cell transplant
questions.explanation: In a frail patient with MDS-EB2 who declines transfusion, the management plan should be individualised. The haematology MDT (option e) is the appropriate forum to consider azacitidine (option a) versus best supportive care (option b) based on performance status and patient choice. Transplant (option c) is contraindicated due to age/comorbidities. Lenalidomide (option d) is for del(5q) only.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman attends her GP with a 3-month history of fatigue and easy bruising. She has no other medical history. Full blood count shows haemoglobin 95 g/L, mean corpuscular volume 106 fL, white cell count 2.8 x10^9/L, and platelet count 70 x10^9/L. Which single finding on the blood film would most raise concern for myelodysplastic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Blast cells in peripheral blood are abnormal and are a key red flag for MDS or acute leukaemia, even at low percentages. Macrocytosis is common in MDS but not specific; hyposegmented neutrophils (Pelger-Huet) can be seen but also in benign conditions; tear drop cells suggest myelofibrosis; rouleaux suggest myeloma.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with known MDS (refractory anaemia with ring sideroblasts, stable for 2 years) presents to the emergency department with a 1-week history of drenching night sweats, bone pain, and new epistaxis. Full blood count shows haemoglobin 80 g/L, white cell count 12 x10^9/L, and platelet count 40 x10^9/L. Which laboratory finding most strongly suggests transformation to acute myeloid leukaemia?
questions.explanation: The WHO definition of AML transformation is ≥20% blasts in blood or bone marrow. This is a red flag requiring urgent admission. Neutrophilia and elevated LDH are non-specific; monocytosis may indicate CMML; elevated ferritin suggests iron overload, not transformation.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old woman with MDS (low risk, transfusion-dependent) develops acute low back pain radiating to both legs, with difficulty walking, and urinary retention over 24 hours. On examination, she has brisk reflexes and extensor plantar responses. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?
options: Vertebral compression fracture due to osteoporosis
Granulocytic sarcoma (chloroma) compressing the spinal cord
questions.explanation: Granulocytic sarcoma is an extramedullary collection of immature cells that can occur in MDS/AML and cause spinal cord compression. Given the acute neurology and absence of trauma or anticoagulation, this is the most likely red flag. Fracture and haematoma are possible but less likely; stenosis is usually chronic; prostate cancer is possible but not MDS-related.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old man with MDS (IPSS-R high risk) is admitted for a first cycle of azacitidine. On day 5 he develops fever, rigors, and hypotension. His blood pressure is 80/50 mmHg, heart rate 120 bpm. Which single investigation result is most consistent with tumour lysis syndrome as a cause of his deterioration?
options: Blood culture positive for Escherichia coli
questions.explanation: Hyperkalaemia is a hallmark of tumour lysis syndrome (TLS), which can occur after chemotherapy for high-risk MDS and is life-threatening. Neutropenia is common in MDS but not specific to TLS; positive culture suggests sepsis; acute kidney injury can be due to TLS or other causes; CRP is non-specific.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman with low-risk MDS receives a 2-unit red cell transfusion for symptomatic anaemia. One hour into the second unit, she develops acute dyspnoea, a bounding pulse, and hypertension (165/95 mmHg). Her temperature is 36.8°C. Which finding on investigation would most strongly differentiate transfusion-associated circulatory overload (TACO) from transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI)?
questions.explanation: TACO is characterised by hydrostatic pulmonary oedema due to volume overload, leading to elevated BNP/NT-proBNP; TRALI typically presents with hypotension and fever, and BNP is not markedly elevated. Both can have bilateral infiltrates and hypoxia; leucocytosis can occur in both; Hb is not discriminatory.
